claim: Everything is a function: all code is a pipeline of functions with discrete inputs and outputs that can be tested and chained; design your inputs and outputs well to get great results
actionable: Break complex agentic systems into composable functions with clear input/output contracts; test and iterate on the functions independently before chaining them
source_title: How to Engineer Multi-Agent Tools: Youtube Metadata Automation

claim: Use the right agent for the job: break your problem into tiny steps, identify whether each needs an agent, code, or manual input, and automate from top to bottom
actionable: Decompose multi-agent workflows from high-level goal down to atomic tasks; determine for each task whether agent, code, or manual effort applies
source_title: How to Engineer Multi-Agent Tools: Youtube Metadata Automation

claim: Follow CRUD in order: agents excel at creating and reading data; avoid using agents for updates and deletions which require more context, awareness, and complexity
actionable: When designing agent workflows, prioritize create and read operations; use traditional code for update and delete operations to minimize risk
source_title: How to Engineer Multi-Agent Tools: Youtube Metadata Automation

claim: Use LLMs to solve problems you understand well and for repetitive tasks with clear inputs/outputs; if you don't understand the problem, you won't know how to prompt effectively
actionable: Before prompting an LLM to automate a task, understand the problem domain deeply; prototype manually first if needed to gain clarity
source_title: How to Engineer Multi-Agent Tools: Youtube Metadata Automation

claim: LLMs are just another function in your pipeline; if you design their inputs well, you'll get great outputs; think of them like you would any code function
actionable: Treat LLM calls as normal functions; invest in clear, specific prompts (inputs) to improve outputs; test and validate LLM outputs like code
source_title: How to Engineer Multi-Agent Tools: Youtube Metadata Automation

claim: Great questions yield great results: formulate clear questions when facing unknowns (What problem am I solving? What is the right tool? What's success?)
actionable: When building agents, document clear success criteria and assumptions; ask explicit questions about problem scope before implementation
source_title: How to Engineer Multi-Agent Tools: Youtube Metadata Automation
